steps.title: Compute k = f(1)
steps.content: For \(k\) to appear as a visible coefficient, the exponent must equal 0 when \(x = 1\) (so the base disappears and the coefficient stands alone). Any form \(A \cdot (1.4)^{x-1}\) gives \(f(1) = A \cdot (1.4)^0 = A\), making \(A = k\) a literal constant.

steps.title: Scan the choices
steps.content:
A: \(50(1.4)^{x+1}\) — at \(x=1\) the exponent is 2, so 50 \(\neq\) k; 50 is not k.
B: \(70(1.4)^x\) — at \(x=1\) the exponent is 1, so \(70 \cdot 1.4 = 98 = k\); the coefficient 70 is not k.
C: \(98(1.4)^{x-1}\) — at \(x=1\) the exponent is 0, so the coefficient 98 equals k directly. ✓
D: \(137.2(1.4)^{x-2}\) — at \(x=1\) the exponent is \(-1\), so \(137.2/1.4 = 98 = k\); the coefficient 137.2 is not k.
That matches choice C — C is correct.
